summary: The texts are one half of the case; the dirt is the other. Across the lands of the story, archaeologists keep pulling the same names and the same events out of the ground — kings, officials, sieges and tunnels the texts had already named, sometimes centuries before the spade confirmed them. This is not proof of every line. It is something quieter and harder to dismiss: again and again, when we can check the ancient world's own account against the physical record, the account holds. Tap any find to see what it confirms.

sources.work: The Convergence — over 200 independent flood accounts
sources.textRecorded: catalogued from Sir James Frazer (1918) through modern comparative databases
sources.provenance: Comparative mythology scholarship; systematic cataloguing across five continents.
sources.quote: In 1918 Sir James George Frazer catalogued flood traditions from over 130 peoples across five continents. Subsequent research has pushed the count past 200. The overlap in specific detail — a warned survivor, a vessel, animals taken aboard, birds released, a mountain landing, a sacrifice afterward — is not universal: peoples who lack the story are the exception. When details this specific match between peoples who could not have communicated, the simplest explanation is a shared source event, not parallel invention.
sources.translation: Summarized from Sir James George Frazer, 'Folk-Lore in the Old Testament,' vol. 1, ch. 4 (London, 1918).
sources.citation: Frazer, Folk-Lore in the Old Testament, vol. 1 (1918); cf. Mark Isaak, 'Flood Stories from Around the World,' TalkOrigins Archive (2002).
sources.notes: The convergence is the argument. No single origin-tradition could account for the breadth; no diffusion model explains peoples with no contact telling the same plot-specifics. The most parsimonious explanation remains that the stories preserve the memory of a real event.

links.label: Frazer, Folk-Lore in the Old Testament (archive)

sources.work: The Sumerian King List — the Flood as historical watershed
sources.traditionEra: 3rd millennium BCE (events); tradition recorded from c. 2100 BCE
sources.textRecorded: oldest copies c. 2100 BCE; roughly 18 copies known from multiple sites
sources.provenance: Mesopotamian administrative and historical tradition; the Weld-Blundell Prism (Ashmolean Museum) is the most complete copy.
sources.quote: Then the Flood swept over the earth. After the Flood had swept over the earth and kingship had descended from heaven a second time, kingship was first in Kish.
sources.translation: After Thorkild Jacobsen, 'The Sumerian King List' (Oriental Institute, University of Chicago, 1939), public domain.
sources.citation: Sumerian King List (ETCSL 2.1.1); Thorkild Jacobsen ed. (Oriental Institute, 1939).
sources.notes: Mesopotamia's own administrative record treats the Flood as a literal chronological dividing line — not mythology, but a watershed placed inside the king-list between named rulers. Before it: kings with impossibly long reigns (the age of the Watchers and giants). After it: kings with human-length reigns. This is the ancient world declaring, in its own words, that the Flood was history.

sources.work: 50 Confirmed Figures — Lawrence Mykytiuk, Biblical Archaeology Review
sources.traditionEra: Iron Age through Roman period (c. 900 BCE – 100 CE)
sources.provenance: Peer-reviewed survey of extra-biblical epigraphic confirmation of named individuals in the Hebrew Bible.
sources.quote: Lawrence Mykytiuk's peer-reviewed survey confirmed more than fifty individuals named in the Hebrew Bible from extra-biblical inscriptions alone — kings, officials, enemies and allies, many confirmed within a century of their own lifetimes, by their own enemies and neighbors. The list spans Israelite and Judean kings, Assyrian and Babylonian rulers who campaigned against them, and individual priests, scribes and officials named in a single chapter of a single book.
sources.translation: Summarized from Lawrence Mykytiuk, 'Archaeology Confirms 50 Real People in the Bible,' Biblical Archaeology Review 40.2 (2014; updated 43.2, 2017).
sources.citation: Lawrence Mykytiuk, Biblical Archaeology Review 40.2 (2014) and 43.2 (2017).
sources.notes: When we can check the ancient texts against physical inscriptions, seals and destruction layers, they consistently name the right people in the right places at the right times. This is not proof of every line of every text; it establishes that ancient sources deserve to be taken seriously as historical testimony — as we already do for Thucydides or Tacitus, whose claims we cannot verify at nearly this rate.

science.intro: The framework behind this book also reads several sacred objects as engineering — specifications precise enough to analyze. Be clear about the category: these are hypotheses, not established findings. They are included because the texts' technical specificity is itself a datum — dimensions, materials, procedures and casualty reports are what engineering documents contain and what campfire stories don't. Each card says what is text, what is physics, and what is conjecture.

evidence.title: The Ark of the Covenant, read as a capacitor
evidence.observation: The text specifies a wooden chest overlaid with gold inside and out (Exodus 25:10–11) — conductor, dielectric, conductor: the anatomy of a capacitor. The reported behaviors match the reading: lethal on casual contact (Uzzah, 2 Samuel 6:6–7), handled safely only by insulated carrying-poles that must never be removed (Exodus 25:13–15), approached by the high priest under strict protocol.
evidence.tie: If you handed these specifications and incident reports to an electrical engineer with the nouns removed, 'high-voltage device' would be a natural reading. That is the framework's claim — not that it is proven, but that the fit is too clean to go unexamined.
evidence.notes: What is text: the dimensions, the two-metal sandwich, the poles, the deaths. What is physics: gold-wood-gold does form a capacitive structure, and a large one in a dry climate can accumulate charge. What is conjecture: capacity, charge source, and purpose — the object is lost, so no measurement is possible and this reading is falsifiable only in replica. Engineers have noted the geometry since the era of Tesla; the card claims the fit, not the verdict.

evidence.title: The Tabernacle, read as a Faraday cage — and the iron rule
evidence.observation: The Tabernacle's walls: an enclosing frame of gold-sheathed boards on metal footings, hung with linen — a conductive enclosure around the Ark's chamber. Alongside it, a strict materials rule: no iron tool on the altar stones (Exodus 20:25; Deuteronomy 27:5), and at the Temple 'neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron' was heard (1 Kings 6:7).
evidence.tie: A conductive shell around a charged object is containment engineering; a prohibition naming one specific metal reads like a materials specification rather than a ritual flourish.
evidence.notes: What is text: the construction sequence and the iron prohibition, repeated across centuries of sources. What is physics: conductive enclosures really do isolate charge and field (Faraday, 1836). What is conjecture: that isolation was the function — the mainstream reading is cultic purity symbolism, and iron's rejection may simply mark the older bronze-age cult resisting the new metal. The framework's point survives the caveat: the specification is precise, repeated, and material-specific, which is at minimum odd.

evidence.title: Jericho — seven days of prescribed sound
evidence.observation: Joshua 6 is written as a procedure: circuits of the wall on a fixed schedule for seven days, massed horns, a prescribed silence, then a single synchronized shout — after which the wall 'fell in its place' (NASB margin: 'fell flat'). Excavation (see the Jericho card in the finds below) shows the revetment collapsed outward, forming a ramp — the opposite of what a siege breach produces.
evidence.tie: The framework reads the seven days as engineering, not liturgy: repeated low-frequency excitation driving a mud-brick wall toward fatigue, finished by a broadband impulse. A wall failing outward, all at once, is what resonant structural failure looks like — and what battering produces never is.
evidence.source: Joshua 6:1–20; Tell es-Sultan excavation reports (Garstang; Kenyon).
evidence.notes: What is text: the schedule, the instruments, the synchronized shout, the outward fall. What is archaeology: the outward-fallen revetment and the burn layer (dating disputed — flagged on the Jericho find-card). What is physics: acoustic fatigue and resonance are real failure modes engineers design against. What is conjecture: that horns and voices could reach the required energy — mainstream archaeology's candidate is an earthquake, which the framework notes would itself need suspiciously precise timing. Hypothesis, labeled as such; the text's procedural specificity is the datum.

evidence.title: Moving the megaliths — the sound-transport legends
evidence.observation: The unliftable stones catalogued in the Giants chapter (Baalbek's ~800-tonne trilithon; the 1,000-tonne quarry block beside it) come wrapped, worldwide, in the same odd detail: colonial-era chroniclers recorded the Andean tradition that the great stones of Tiahuanaco 'came through the air to the sound of a trumpet'; Maya legend has Uxmal's pyramid raised by a whistling dwarf; Geoffrey of Monmouth has Merlin bring the Giants' Dance (Stonehenge) by art where force had failed.
evidence.tie: Peoples who never met attached the same mechanism — sound — to the same problem: stones too big for muscle. The framework treats that convergence the way this book treats every convergence: as a memory worth taking seriously.
evidence.source: Baalbek trilithon; Tiahuanaco traditions (colonial chronicles); Geoffrey of Monmouth, Historia Regum Britanniae VIII.
evidence.notes: What is data: the stones (measure them), and the legends (read them). What is physics: acoustic levitation is real and demonstrated — laboratories routinely float small objects on ultrasound standing waves — but no known scaling reaches building stones; the gap is many orders of magnitude. What is conjecture: everything between the lab bench and the trilithon. This is the framework's most speculative card and is labeled accordingly; it earns its place because the sound-motif convergence in the legends is real and independently attested, whatever explains it.
